summary: I came to Florida from Virginia in the early 1880s and settled on an orange grove near Picolata. Back then, Florida's St. Johns River valley was the center of the citrus industry, and we raised oranges, sugarcane, and sweet potatoes. Over time, I witnessed the depletion of Florida's timber and the decline of the sawmill business. Later, I moved to Jacksonville in 1893, which was then a small but growing town.

I originally came from Virginia before moving to Florida in the early 1880s. I was about twenty-two years old when my family and I settled on an orange grove near Picolata. Leaving Virginia marked the start of my life in the citrus growing region along the St. Johns River. It was quite a change from my previous home.

My family settled near Picolata, a prosperous river town on the banks of the St. Johns River, about 18 miles from St. Augustine. We lived in a large frame house on an established orange grove. The town was a hub for river traffic, with daily boats bringing supplies and shipping fruit to Atlantic ports. Over time, the old landing at Picolata faded away, and now where our home stood is a turpentine still.

The St. Johns River was central to our life in Florida, especially for shipping oranges and other produce. It was bustling with passenger and freight boats like the Jelin Sweeter and Sylvan Glen running daily between Jacksonville and Palatka. The river was also rich in fish and waterfowl, with ducks settling by the acre and fishermen hauling in large catches. I remember the aquatic plants too, like water lettuce before hyacinths took over.

Our orange grove near Picolata was about 18 miles from St. Augustine. Traveling to St. Augustine took about three hours by buggy over rough sand and corduroy roads. It was the nearest city of note, but transportation was slow and difficult in those days. The proximity to St. Augustine connected us to larger markets and services.

Interestingly, the crates we used to pack our oranges were made from birchwood shipped all the way from Maine. It was curious that such material, favored by cabinet makers and builders, was used for packing fruit in Florida. This connection shows how far our products traveled and the interdependence of regions even back then.

Our oranges were shipped to several Atlantic ports, including Charleston, South Carolina. This was part of the daily shipping service that connected Florida's citrus growers to markets up the coast. Charleston was one of the key destinations for our fruit in those early days before railroads extended south of Jacksonville.

Baltimore was another important Atlantic port where our Florida oranges were shipped. Along with Charleston and New York, it formed part of the network that distributed Florida citrus to northern markets. This trade was vital before railroads reached further south in Florida.

New York City was one of the major destinations for our orange shipments from Florida. The daily boat service on the St. Johns River helped get our fruit to northern cities like New York before railroads were established south of Jacksonville. It was impressive to see how far our produce traveled by water.

I moved to Jacksonville in 1893, when it was still a small but growing town made up of several municipalities like East Jacksonville and Lavilla. I set up my business on East Duval Street, right across from St. Luke's Hospital, which was run by prominent women including Mrs. Alexander Mitchell. Jacksonville was a sawmill town then, with timber coming from nearby areas like Middleburg. The streets were sandy and the town felt very neighborly compared to today.

Palatka was a key river town where the parents of a laborer I knew came from during a medical emergency. It was also one of the destinations served by daily passenger boats running from Jacksonville to Palatka on the St. Johns River. Palatka was part of the network that connected river towns and supported the citrus industry.

Crescent City was one of the stops for freight boats like the Water Lily running from Jacksonville. It was part of the river shipping route along the St. Johns that helped move goods including our citrus and other crops. The river traffic was vital for commerce before railroads expanded in the region.

Sanford was the farthest point on the St. Johns River that river shipping reached, served by boats like the City of Jacksonville which carried both freight and passengers. This made Sanford an important terminus for river commerce in the late 19th century. Our orange shipments sometimes traveled as far as Sanford before being distributed further.

Green Cove Springs was about nine miles from Picolata and had the nearest doctor I could call on during emergencies. Captain Tillie Hallowes of Green Cove Springs commanded the Sylvan Glen, one of the prominent passenger boats on the St. Johns River. The town was also a source of timber and had sawmills serving the region.

Tallahassee was one of the places notified during a medical emergency involving a laborer with tetanus, but communication and transportation were slow in those days. It was also connected by the Seaboard Air Line Railroad which ran from Fernandina to Lake City and Tallahassee. The town was part of the broader network of Florida's rail and river commerce in the late 19th century.

Chattahoochee was another town notified during the tetanus case of a laborer, but due to slow mail and limited transportation, help arrived late. It was part of the regional network of authorities involved in health and legal matters in Florida at the time. The experience showed how isolated rural communities were in the 1880s and 1890s.

Middleburg was one of the nearby areas where I obtained timber for fuel and building materials after moving to Jacksonville. The region was heavily wooded and supplied much of the wood used in the city before coal became more common. Timber from Middleburg helped support Jacksonville's sawmill industry in the late 19th century.

The Seaboard Air Line Railroad ran from Fernandina to Lake City, Tallahassee, and Cedar Keys, with a branch into Jacksonville. When I came to Jacksonville, the old tracks could still be seen at the foot of Hogan Street near the Logan Coal Company yards. The railroad was an important development that complemented river traffic and helped connect Florida's towns and cities.

Hogan Street in Jacksonville was near where the Seaboard Air Line Railroad tracks ended, close to the Logan Coal Company yards. When I arrived in 1893, the town was mostly confined between Hogan Street and Hogan's Creek. It was part of the core area of Jacksonville before the city expanded and modernized.

Hogan's Creek was a geographic boundary in Jacksonville, with the town largely confined between it and Hogan Street when I first arrived. It helped define the early layout of the city before it grew into the sprawling metropolis it is today. The area was part of the community where I established my business on East Duval Street nearby.

I established my business on East Duval Street in Jacksonville when I arrived in 1893. Across from my office was the early St. Luke's Hospital, which was started and run by prominent women of the town. East Duval was a central street in the small but growing Jacksonville community of that era.

St. Luke's Hospital in Jacksonville was started, financed, and run by the women of the town, including Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, the wife of a wealthy railroad executive. It was located across from my business on East Duval Street when I arrived in 1893. The hospital represented the community spirit and the important role women played in healthcare at the time.

Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, the wife of a wealthy railroad executive, was one of the prominent women who started and ran St. Luke's Hospital in Jacksonville. Her involvement highlighted the leadership role women took in healthcare and community service during the late 19th century. The Mitchell family was well known in Jacksonville society.

Dr. Wallace Mitchell was one of two prominent physicians in Jacksonville when I arrived in the early 1890s. He was well educated, having trained in the United States and abroad, and was known for being very busy with a large medical practice. Like his brother Neal, he was among the first to use the telephone to stay in constant contact with his office, but sadly both died young from overwork.

Dr. Neal Mitchell, brother to Wallace Mitchell, was also a leading physician in Jacksonville in the early 1890s. He shared a similar background of extensive education and a busy practice, and was among the first doctors to use the telephone for office communication. Both brothers worked tirelessly and passed away young due to the strain of their work.
